reasoning: Method A's strongest novel element is the two-stream verification head with a low-rank FFN-activity predictor for early screening of candidate blocks before full verification — this partial-pass early abort mechanism combined with a Picard-style horizon split for multi-sequence scheduling is a somewhat distinctive combination. However, blockwise parallel verification with cached KV states and beam search over draft candidates are well-established ideas in speculative decoding literature. Method B's strongest novel element is the explicit construction of a lookahead tree with multi-depth draft heads trained via a coverage loss (encouraging D's top-b sets to contain L's argmax), combined with an exact sampling procedure that follows sampled tokens through precomputed tree logits — this tree-based speculation with coverage-aware distillation and online adaptation of tree depth/width is a more coherent and distinctive architectural contribution. The coverage loss and multi-depth head training objective for maximizing tree hit-rate represent a more original training-time mechanism not commonly found in prior speculative decoding work.

Method A's strongest novel element is the two-pass "probe" architecture combining prompt-conditioned shallow lookahead attention with Nyström leverage scores and spatial-frequency (Laplacian energy) measures into a weighted importance score, plus structure-preserving coarsening with per-tile quotas. These are interesting combinations but each component (attention-based token scoring, leverage scores, greedy graph merging) exists in prior work on token pruning/merging (e.g., ToMe, EViT, DynamicViT). Method B's strongest novel element is the contrastive two-forward-pass approach—running the decoder with and without visual cross-attention and using the KL/Hellinger divergence between the resulting distributions as a prompt dependency signal to weight visual token importance. This "counterfactual" attribution mechanism for scoring visual tokens is a more original framing not commonly seen in visual token pruning literature. Additionally, the dynamic budget decay during decoding and the SVD/eigenvector-based "viewer token" summarization add further originality. Compared to Method A's weighted combination of established signals, Method B's counterfactual prompt-dependency profiling represents a harder-to-find prior-work combination.

Method A's strongest novel element is the combination of a recurrent refinement block with a learned halting policy (inspired by Adaptive Computation Time) applied at the token level during autoregressive decoding, augmented with a speculative agreement guardrail and a bounded re-entry window for post-hoc correction. The core ACT-style pondering mechanism applied to decoding is not entirely new in concept, though the specific combination with distillation targets, dual-variable budget control, and re-entry windows adds some originality. Method B's strongest novel element is the joint controller that simultaneously sets three orthogonal compute knobs (layer depth via early exit, beam width, and verification scope) per decoding step, trained offline with calibrated risk guarantees via isotonic regression. While each individual component (early exit, dynamic beam, verification) exists in prior work, the unified controller jointly optimizing all three with formal risk calibration is a more distinctive combination. Both methods are largely assemblies of known components, but Method B's three-knob joint controller with calibrated risk guarantees is a more distinctive framing not easily found as a unified prior work.
